title: Enhancing supercurrent-based inertial sensing via interactions in atomtronic angular accelerometers
authors: S. Carmona-López, A. Matos-Abiague, F. Isaule, L. Morales-Molina
abstract: We theoretically investigate supercurrents of ultracold atoms in angularly ac-shaken ring lattices subjected to external rotation. Our results demonstrate how these supercurrents can be harnessed for the development of high-precision atomtronic angular accelerometers. Using both analytical and numerical approaches within the Bose-Hubbard model framework, we demonstrate that a significant net atomic current arises when the lattice driving frequency is tuned to an integer fraction of the Bloch frequency, while the current averages to nearly zero away from such a resonance. In the single-particle regime, the resonance width scales inversely with the averaging time, thereby setting a fundamental Fourier-limited bound on the measurement's sensitivity. Strikingly, our numerical simulations demonstrate that this Fourier limit - a fundamental barrier in the non-interacting system - can be surpassed by introducing weak interactions between atoms. In the interacting regime, the sensitivity surpasses the Fourier-limited scaling with the averaging time, achieving an improvement of at least two orders of magnitude over the single-particle scenario, and exceeding the performance of previously proposed ultracold-atom-based angular accelerometers. These findings pave the way for developing new atomic-current-based inertial sensors with interaction-enhanced sensitivity.
summary: This paper shows that weak interactions between ultracold atoms in a shaken ring lattice can significantly enhance the sensitivity of angular accelerometers. By inducing dephasing-induced interference, interactions narrow the resonance peaks, allowing the sensor to surpass the fundamental sensitivity limits of non-interacting systems.
main_problem: The goal is to develop high-precision atomtronic angular accelerometers and overcome the fundamental Fourier-limited sensitivity bound present in non-interacting (single-particle) systems.
main_result: Introducing weak interatomic interactions enables the system to surpass the Fourier limit, achieving a resonance peak narrowing that improves sensitivity by at least two orders of magnitude compared to the single-particle regime.
method: The study uses a combination of analytical methods (Jacobi-Anger expansion, Floquet theory, and Green's function formalism) and numerical simulations within the Bose-Hubbard framework.
model_or_system: The system consists of ultracold atoms in a 1D angularly ac-shaken ring optical lattice (toroidal trap) described by the Bose-Hubbard model, including hopping, on-site interactions, and a time-dependent Peierls phase.
key_observables: Atomic supercurrent (both instantaneous and time-averaged) and the Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of the resonance peak.
important_parameters: Angular acceleration (alpha), driving frequency (omega), interaction strength (U/J), measurement time (tau), and driving amplitude (A).
main_assumptions: Tight-binding approximation; neglecting high-frequency oscillating terms; small detuning regime; and the assumption that all particles initially occupy the zero-momentum condensate state.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup; Fig 2 illustrates resonance profiles under different phase conditions; Fig 3 shows subharmonic resonances and the effect of measurement time; Fig 5 compares interacting vs non-interacting current; Fig 6 shows the quasienergy spectrum and avoided crossings; Fig 7 plots sensitivity and current decay vs interaction strength; Fig 8 shows sensitivity scaling.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the atomtronic sensor concept, develops the analytical framework for the non-interacting regime, extends the model to include weak interactions using Green's functions, provides numerical validation of sensitivity enhancement, and discusses the experimental trade-offs between sensitivity and signal strength.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a way to use many-body effects (interactions) to break a fundamental single-particle precision limit (the Fourier limit) in quantum metrology, which is highly relevant for the development of next-generation inertial sensors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
